meta.name: SAS Zung Self-Rating Anxiety Scale
meta.description: The SAS is a 20-item self-report scale developed by William W.K. Zung to assess the severity of anxiety symptoms. It covers both psychological and somatic manifestations of anxiety and is widely used in clinical and research settings.
meta.author: Zung (1971)
meta.reference: Zung WWK. A rating instrument for anxiety disorders. Psychosomatics. 1971;12(6):371-379.
meta.instruction: Please read each statement and decide how much of the time the statement describes how you have been feeling during the past several days.

commonChoices.label: Most or all of the time

questions.text: I feel more nervous and anxious than usual
I feel afraid for no reason at all
I get upset easily or feel panicky
I feel like I'm falling apart and going to pieces
I feel that everything is all right and nothing bad will happen
My arms and legs shake and tremble
I am bothered by headaches, neck and back pains
I feel weak and get tired easily
I feel calm and can sit still easily
I can feel my heart beating fast
I am bothered by dizzy spells
I have fainting spells or feel like it
I can breathe in and out easily
I get feelings of numbness and tingling in my fingers and toes
I am bothered by stomachaches or indigestion
I have to empty my bladder often
My hands are usually dry and warm
My face gets hot and blushes
I fall asleep easily and get a good night's rest
I have nightmares

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your anxiety score is within the normal range, with no clinically significant anxiety symptoms detected. Your current level of nervousness and worry falls within healthy limits and does not suggest an anxiety disorder.
suggestions: Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle with regular physical exercise and balanced nutrition
Practice relaxation techniques such as deep breathing or meditation as part of your general wellness routine
Keep up regular social activities and connections to support your emotional well-being

ranges.label: Mild Anxiety
ranges.description: Your score suggests mild anxiety symptoms that may be affecting your daily comfort and functioning. You may notice increased nervousness, physical tension, or worry that occasionally interferes with concentration or sleep.
suggestions: Learn and regularly practice relaxation techniques such as deep breathing and progressive muscle relaxation
Maintain a consistent physical exercise routine, as regular activity is one of the most effective natural anxiety reducers
Reduce caffeine and stimulant intake, as these substances can significantly amplify anxiety symptoms
If symptoms persist for more than two weeks, consider scheduling a consultation with a healthcare professional

ranges.label: Moderate Anxiety
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate anxiety symptoms that may be significantly affecting your daily functioning, work performance, and interpersonal relationships. Both psychological and physical symptoms of anxiety are likely present.
suggestions: Consult a mental health professional such as a therapist or psychiatrist for a thorough evaluation and treatment plan
Cognitive behavioral therapy has strong research evidence as an effective treatment for anxiety disorders
Maintain a structured daily routine including consistent sleep, meal, and activity schedules for stability
Practice daily relaxation exercises and consider incorporating mindfulness meditation into your routine
Avoid self-medication with alcohol, sedatives, or other substances, as they often worsen anxiety over time

ranges.label: Severe Anxiety
ranges.description: Your score suggests severe anxiety symptoms that are likely causing major impairment in your daily life and overall functioning. Immediate professional evaluation and treatment are strongly recommended at this severity level.
suggestions: Seek immediate consultation with a psychiatrist or mental health professional for urgent evaluation
A combination of medication and psychotherapy is often the most effective approach for severe anxiety
Ensure you have a reliable support system of family and friends who understand your situation
Learn to recognize the physical signs of panic attacks and practice grounding techniques to manage acute episodes
